title: Tamil Kathirikai Gothsu

ingredients.name: large brinjal (baingan)
visual_checks: each about 200 g; roast whole over direct flame
ingredients.notes: each about 200 g; roast whole over direct flame

subs.name: raw banana (vazhakkai), boiled and mashed — no flame-roasting needed, different flavour
juice of half a lemon added off the heat for a brighter, less complex acidity

steps.text: Place the whole brinjals directly over a gas flame on medium-high. Rotate every minute using tongs until the entire skin is charred and the brinjal collapses and feels soft when pressed, about 10 minutes. The smoky aroma is what makes this dish. Alternatively, roast under a grill or in a 220°C oven for 20 minutes.

Once cool enough to handle, peel off and discard all the charred skin. Mash the cooked flesh coarsely with a fork — leave some texture, do not purée. Set aside. Soak the tamarind in 50 ml warm water, extract the pulp.
Heat oil in a kadai over medium flame. Splutter mustard seeds, then add curry leaves, shallots, and green chillies. Cook until the shallots turn golden, about 4 minutes.
Add the tomatoes with a pinch of salt and cook until they collapse and the oil separates, about 4 minutes. Add chilli powder, coriander powder, and turmeric. Stir for 30 seconds on low heat.

Add the mashed brinjal and tamarind extract. Stir everything together and simmer on medium for 8 minutes until the gothsu thickens to a chunky, spreadable consistency. Check salt and stir in coriander leaves.
Serve hot or at room temperature alongside ven pongal, idli, or as a side to rice. The gothsu keeps well for a day and the smoky flavour deepens as it rests.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: brinjal → raw banana (vazhakkai), boiled and mashed — no flame-roasting needed, different flavour; tamarind → juice of half a lemon added off the heat for a brighter, less complex acidity; shallot → 1 medium red onion, finely chopped
